Number 9.
the thermal death. A rocky planet's long -term habitability depends on its internal heat engine.
Radioactive decay in the mantle drives convection, which drives volcanism, which recycles gases
into the atmosphere, which maintains surface pressure and temperature. Earth's mantle is still hot enough to
sustain this cycle 4 .5 billion years after formation. The thermal engine runs because
the planet is massive enough to retain heat over geological time. Mars has 11 % of
Earth's mass. That is not a minor difference. It is the difference between a planet that holds its heat for 10
billion years and one that begins cooling within its first billion. Mars's smaller volume gave it a
higher surface area to volume ratio, meaning heat radiated into space faster than the interior could replace
it. The mantle cooled, convection slowed. Volcanism concentrated into fewer and fewer
eruption sites instead of spreading across tectonic boundaries the way it does on Earth.
Olympus Mons, the largest volcano in the solar system at 21 kilometers tall, three times the height of

energy to crack its crust open anywhere else. Mars was too small to stay warm, and a cold planet
is a dead planet. The thermal problem explains why Mars lost its engine. This next theory explains
what it lost the ability to do.
Number 8.

rocks. Our And forms carbonate minerals that are eventually subducted back into the mantle through plate

the cycle begins again. This is the carbon -silicate cycle, and it is the only known mechanism capable
of stabilizing a rocky planet's atmosphere over billions of years. Mars never developed plate tectonics.
The planet's lithosphere formed a single, unbroken shell. Without subduction, carbonates that formed
on the surface stayed on the surface. CO2 that was pulled out of the atmosphere was never returned.
The planet's thermostat broke because there was no recycling mechanism to keep it running.
Orbital instruments have searched for the massive carbonate deposits that this process should have left behind.
They found almost none. This is the missing carbonate paradox, and it remains one of the most
troubling open questions in Martian geology. Either the carbonates were destroyed by acidic conditions on the surface...

Number 5.
dynamo collapse. Mars had a global magnetic field generated by convection in its liquid iron core,
the dynamo process that produces Earth's today. Crustal rocks on Mars carry the magnetic
of that ancient field locked into the mineral at the moment they cooled billions of years ago.
The dynamo was active as early as 4 .5 billion years ago, and persisted until somewhere between 4 .2
and 3 .7 billion years ago. Then it stopped. Research from the University of Tokyo, published in
Nature Communications, simulated the conditions Mars' core using iron -sulfur -hydrogen alloys
at extreme pressures. The team found that as Mars cooled, the two liquid components of the
core, iron -sulfur and iron -hydrogen, I separated into distinct layers.
This separation initially drove convection, powering the dynamo. But once the liquids fully stratified,
convection ceased. The currents stopped. The magnetic field vanished. Not weakened.
Vanished. Earth's core temperature is approximately 6 ,000 degrees Celsius. At that the
iron and iron -hydrogen liquids remain mixed. Earth's core is only stratified its very top.
Mars' core, smaller and cooler, stratified entirely. The dynamo did not wind down gradually the

But something from outside may have helped silence it.
Number 4.

and persisted for days before condensing. That was one impact. Mars at least four confirmed

The bombardment ensured it would never recover. The impacts broke the surface. The core broke the shield.
And then the water began to leave.

aligned_segments.title: The orbital destabilization
aligned_segments.body:
Number ten. The orbital destabilization.
Mars should orbit the sun on a near-circular path, the way Earth does. A stable, predictable ellipse with minimal seasonal variation. That is what the standard accretion model predicts for a rocky planet at 1.5 astronomical units from a G-type star. The orbit should be calm. The climate should be moderated by geometry alone. Every simulation of planetary formation in this region of the solar system produces a world with low eccentricity and steady insolation across its year. Mars does not follow that prediction. The planet's orbital eccentricity is 0.0934, nearly six times greater than Earth's 0.0167. That means Mars swings 42 million kilometers closer to the sun at perihelion than at aphelion. For comparison, that distance is roughly the entire orbital radius of Mercury. The result is a planet that receives 44 percent more solar energy at its closest approach than at its farthest, producing seasonal extremes that no atmosphere can fully buffer. Jupiter's gravitational influence, tugging on Mars across billions of orbital cycles, stretched the orbit into this elongated shape and kept it there. Mars did not choose instability. The largest planet in the solar system imposed it. And an unstable orbit means an unstable climate, compounding every other process that was already pulling Mars apart.
That was one piece of geometry. The next theory is about something far more fundamental. The size of the planet itself.

aligned_segments.body:
Number nine. The thermal death.
A rocky planet's long-term habitability depends on its internal heat engine. Radioactive decay in the mantle drives convection, which drives volcanism, which recycles gases into the atmosphere, which maintains surface pressure and temperature. Earth's mantle is still hot enough to sustain this cycle 4.5 billion years after formation. The thermal engine runs because the planet is massive enough to retain heat over geological time. Mars has 11 percent of Earth's mass. That is not a minor difference. It is the difference between a planet that holds its heat for ten billion years and one that begins cooling within its first billion. Mars' smaller volume gave it a higher surface-area-to-volume ratio, meaning heat radiated into space faster than the interior could replace it. The mantle cooled. Convection slowed. Volcanism concentrated into fewer and fewer eruption sites instead of spreading across tectonic boundaries the way it does on Earth. Olympus Mons, the largest volcano in the solar system at 21 kilometers tall, three times the height of Everest, is not evidence of a geologically active world. It is evidence of a dying one. A single hotspot punching through a thickening, stiffening lithosphere because the planet no longer had enough thermal energy to crack its crust open anywhere else. Mars was too small to stay warm. And a cold planet is a dead planet.
The thermal problem explains why Mars lost its engine. This next theory explains what it lost the ability to do.

aligned_segments.title: The carbonate failure
aligned_segments.body:
Number eight. The carbonate failure.
On Earth, atmospheric carbon dioxide dissolves in rainwater, reacts with silicate rocks, and forms carbonate minerals that are eventually subducted back into the mantle through plate tectonics. The mantle heats those carbonates, releases the CO2 through volcanic outgassing, and the cycle begins again. This is the carbon-silicate cycle, and it is the only known mechanism capable of stabilizing a rocky planet's atmosphere over billions of years. Mars never developed plate tectonics. The planet's lithosphere formed a single, unbroken shell. Without subduction, carbonates that formed on the surface stayed on the surface. CO2 that was pulled out of the atmosphere was never returned. The planet's thermostat broke because there was no recycling mechanism to keep it running. Orbital instruments have searched for the massive carbonate deposits that this process should have left behind. They found almost none. This is the missing carbonate paradox, and it remains one of the most troubling open questions in Martian geology. Either the carbonates were destroyed by acidic conditions on the surface, or Mars lost so much of its atmosphere so quickly that the carbon cycle never operated at the scale required to leave a geological signature. Either answer describes a planet that could not regulate itself. Earth's atmosphere is not stable because Earth is special. It is stable because Earth recycles. Mars could not recycle. So the atmosphere it started with was the only atmosphere it would ever have. And it was not enough.
That was a planet that could not regulate its own chemistry. This next one is about a planet that could not even keep its own axis stable.

aligned_segments.title: The obliquity chaos
aligned_segments.body:
Number seven. The obliquity chaos.
A planet's axial tilt determines how sunlight distributes across its surface. Earth's tilt holds steady at approximately 23.4 degrees, stabilized by the gravitational influence of the Moon. This stability means predictable seasons, consistent polar ice coverage, and a climate system that changes slowly enough for biology to adapt. Mars has no large stabilizing moon. Its axial tilt currently sits at about 25 degrees, similar to Earth's. But simulations from the Laboratoire d'Astrophysique de Marseille show that over millions of years, Mars' obliquity swings chaotically between 10 and 60 degrees. At 60 degrees, the poles face the sun directly. Ice sublimates into the atmosphere from the polar caps and migrates toward the equator. At 10 degrees, the opposite happens. The poles freeze permanently and the atmosphere thins as CO2 locks into polar ice. These are not gentle transitions. They are climate whiplash events that redistribute the planet's entire volatile inventory on timescales too fast for any geological process to compensate. The Tharsis volcanic province, a bulge of volcanic material roughly the size of North America and massing approximately 300 million billion metric tons, sits off-center on Mars' equator. Its asymmetric mass loading contributed to the planet's rotational instability, amplifying the obliquity swings that were already occurring. Mars was not just tilting. It was being pulled off-balance by the weight of its own geological history. A planet that cannot hold a stable axial tilt cannot hold a stable climate. And a planet that cannot hold a stable climate cannot hold liquid water on its surface for the billions of years that complex chemistry requires.
The tilt was unstable. The orbit was unstable. And then the shield came down.

aligned_segments.title: The solar stripping
aligned_segments.body:
Number six. The solar stripping.
The sun emits a continuous stream of charged particles traveling at roughly 1.6 million kilometers per hour. On Earth, the magnetosphere deflects this stream around the planet. The atmosphere remains intact. On Mars, after the magnetic field collapsed, the solar wind hit the upper atmosphere directly. NASA's MAVEN orbiter, which has been studying Martian atmospheric escape since 2014, measured the current rate of loss at approximately 100 grams per second. That is a quarter pound of atmosphere bleeding into space every single second you are watching this sentence. At current rates, the loss is modest. But 3.5 billion years ago, the young sun was up to 100 times more active in ultraviolet output. Solar storms were more frequent and more violent. MAVEN data showed that during a single solar storm in March 2015, atmospheric escape rates increased by a factor of 10 to 20. Scale that to the conditions of the early solar system and the math becomes devastating. The stripping rate was not 100 grams per second. It was kilograms. Possibly tens of kilograms. Every second, for hundreds of millions of years. The solar wind did not erode Mars' atmosphere the way wind erodes rock. It tore it away the way fire consumes paper. Fast. Violent. Irreversible. And the only thing that could have prevented it was the one thing Mars had already lost.
That was what happened after the shield fell. This next theory is about why the shield fell in the first place.

aligned_segments.title: The dynamo collapse
aligned_segments.body:
Number five. The dynamo collapse.
Mars once had a global magnetic field generated by convection in its liquid iron core, the same dynamo process that produces Earth's magnetosphere today. Crustal rocks on Mars still carry the magnetic signature of that ancient field, locked into the minerals at the moment they cooled billions of years ago. The dynamo was active as early as 4.5 billion years ago and persisted until somewhere between 4.2 and 3.7 billion years ago. Then it stopped. Research from the University of Tokyo, published in Nature Communications, simulated the conditions inside Mars' core using iron-sulfur-hydrogen alloys at extreme pressures. The team found that as Mars cooled, the two liquid components of the core, iron-sulfur and iron-hydrogen, separated into distinct layers. This separation initially drove convection, powering the dynamo. But once the liquids fully stratified, convection ceased. The currents stopped. The magnetic field vanished. Not weakened. Vanished. Earth's core temperature is approximately 6,000 degrees Celsius. At that temperature, the iron-sulfur and iron-hydrogen liquids remain mixed. Earth's core is only stratified at its very top. Mars' core, smaller and cooler, stratified entirely. The dynamo did not wind down gradually the way a battery drains. It hit a physical threshold and switched off. The magnetic field protecting the atmosphere, the oceans, and any possibility of surface life disappeared because the planet's core got cold enough for two liquids to separate. A chemical phase transition in a ball of iron 3,400 kilometers below the surface sentenced everything above it to death.
The core went quiet on its own. But something from outside may have helped silence it.

aligned_segments.title: The great bombardment
aligned_segments.body:
Number four. The great bombardment.
Between 4.1 and 3.8 billion years ago, the inner solar system endured a period of intensified asteroid impacts driven by the gravitational migration of Jupiter and Saturn. Mars bore the full force of this bombardment. The Hellas impact basin in Mars' southern hemisphere spans 2,300 kilometers in diameter and plunges over 7 kilometers below the mean surface level. The object that created it was approximately 370 kilometers wide. When it struck, the entire surface of Mars was heated by hundreds of degrees. Seventy meters of molten rock fell across the planet. An atmosphere of vaporized stone, ten times denser than Earth's current atmosphere, formed and persisted for days before condensing. That was one impact. Mars endured at least four confirmed giant basin-forming impacts during this period, including Argyre, Isidis, and Utopia. Computational models show that impacts of this magnitude inject enough thermal energy into the mantle to disrupt the heat gradient between the core and the mantle. That gradient is what drives core convection. Disrupt it, and the dynamo falters. The Late Heavy Bombardment did not just scar Mars' surface. It may have delivered the thermal shock that pushed a weakening dynamo past the point of no return. The planet was already cooling. The bombardment ensured it would never recover.
The impacts broke the surface. The core broke the shield. And then the water began to leave.

aligned_segments.title: The water spiral
aligned_segments.body:
Number three.
The water spiral.
Mars had liquid water. Curiosity rover found an ancient lake bed in Gale Crater with sedimentary layers indicating standing water persisted for millions of years. The chemistry of that lake bed contained carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, nitrogen, phosphorus, and sulfur, every element required for microbial life as we understand it. The European Space Agency's Mars Express orbiter detected a subsurface body of liquid water beneath the southern polar ice cap roughly 20 kilometers wide. Water was not scarce on Mars. It was abundant. But water vapor in the upper atmosphere is vulnerable to solar ultraviolet radiation, which splits H2O molecules into hydrogen and oxygen. Hydrogen, being the lightest element, escapes the planet's gravity entirely. The oxygen either escapes with the solar wind or reacts with iron in the surface rocks, producing the iron oxide that gives Mars its red color. Every water molecule that reached the upper atmosphere was destroyed. The hydrogen left the planet forever. The oxygen rusted the ground. As water vapor disappeared from the atmosphere, the greenhouse effect weakened. Temperatures dropped. Surface water froze. Frozen water does not evaporate into the atmosphere, so the greenhouse weakened further. Temperatures dropped further. More water froze. This is a positive feedback loop with no natural exit. Each stage of water loss made the next stage of water loss more severe. Mars did not run out of water because the water was consumed. It ran out of water because the loss mechanism accelerated itself until the surface froze solid and the atmosphere thinned to the point where liquid water became physically impossible. The surface pressure on Mars today is 6.1 millibars. Sea level pressure on Earth is 1,013 millibars. At 6.1 millibars, water cannot exist as a liquid at any temperature. It sublimates directly from ice to vapor. Mars crossed that threshold billions of years ago, and nothing in the planet's physics can bring it back.

aligned_segments.title: The cascade convergence
aligned_segments.body:
Number two. The cascade convergence.
No single theory on this list explains Mars. The magnetic field collapse alone does not account for the atmospheric loss. The solar wind stripping alone does not explain the speed of the water disappearance. The small planet thermal problem alone does not predict the timing. The orbital instability alone does not produce the surface we observe. But together, they form a single interconnected death sequence. The planet cooled because it was too small. The cooling stratified the core, which killed the dynamo. The dynamo's death exposed the atmosphere to the solar wind. The solar wind stripped the atmosphere, which reduced the greenhouse effect. The reduced greenhouse froze the surface water. The frozen water could not evaporate to maintain atmospheric pressure. The falling pressure made liquid water impossible. The obliquity chaos redistributed what little volatile inventory remained. The bombardment accelerated the core failure that was already in progress. Every theory on this list is a chapter in the same story, and the story is a cascade. One failure created the conditions for the next failure, which created the conditions for the next, until the planet crossed a threshold from which recovery was impossible. The cascade did not require a single catastrophic event. It required only that enough systems fail in sequence to push the planet past the point where any individual system could compensate. Mars did not die from one wound. It died because every wound made the next wound worse, and the planet had no mechanism to stop the bleeding. The convergence is what makes Mars terrifying. Because cascades do not require rare events. They require only that a planet be slightly too small, slightly too far from its star, and slightly too unlucky in its neighborhood. The margin between a living world and a dead one is not wide. It is razor-thin.

aligned_segments.title: The habitability decay
aligned_segments.body:
Number one. The habitability decay.
Every rocky planet in the habitable zone of a main-sequence star begins with internal heat, a magnetic dynamo, volcanic outgassing, and the potential for liquid water. These are not gifts. They are temporary conditions produced by the thermal energy of accretion and radioactive decay. And they are all on a timer. The heat dissipates. The core cools. The dynamo weakens. The atmosphere thins. The water leaves. Mars ran through this sequence in approximately one billion years because its mass was too low to sustain the thermal engine any longer. Earth has not run through it yet because Earth is larger and retains heat more efficiently. But the physics is identical. Earth's core is cooling at a measurable rate. Earth's magnetic field has weakened by approximately 9 percent over the last 200 years. The mechanisms that killed Mars are the same mechanisms operating inside the planet you are standing on right now. The habitable zone is not a permanent address. It is a window. A temporary alignment of thermal, magnetic, atmospheric, and orbital conditions that allows liquid water to persist on a rocky surface for a finite period. Mars entered that window at roughly the same time Earth did. Mars left it 3.5 billion years ago. Earth is still inside it. But the window does not stay open because a planet deserves it. It stays open only as long as the physics allows. And the physics, on every rocky world ever observed, trends in exactly one direction. The planet next door is not a mystery. It is a preview.
The universe did not kill Mars with malice. It killed Mars with physics. The same physics that currently keeps you alive, that heats your core, that holds your atmosphere, that shields your water from the void. The difference between Earth and Mars is not a difference in kind. It is a difference in timing. Mars is what every rocky planet becomes when the heat runs out and the shield comes down and the atmosphere scatters into the vacuum. You can see it in the night sky with the naked eye. A small, red, frozen point of light that once had oceans. That is not a distant tragedy. That is a clock.
